planting.note: Sow fresh seed outdoors in fall to allow natural cold stratification over winter, or cold-stratify seeds for 60–90 days before spring sowing; plant in lean, dry, rocky, or sandy soil in full sun — russet buffaloberry fixes nitrogen via root nodules and thrives in poor soils where other fruiting shrubs struggle.

directions: Sow seeds in fall directly in the garden or cold-stratify for 60–90 days before planting in spring; plant in dry, rocky, or sandy soil with excellent drainage in full sun, as russet buffaloberry naturally colonizes forest edges, rocky slopes, and open woodlands from Alaska to the northeastern United States. This dioecious nitrogen-fixer requires both male and female plants to produce its small, tart red berries, which ripen in midsummer. The berries were a prized food source for many indigenous peoples and can be whipped with a small amount of water and a touch of sweetener into a stable foam traditionally called 'sxusem' or Indian ice cream. Established plants are extremely cold-hardy and drought-tolerant once rooted, making russet buffaloberry an excellent low-maintenance native for challenging northern and high-altitude sites.

planting.note: Sow cold-stratified seed in fall or early spring in well-drained, lean soil in full sun; willow-leaved sea buckthorn is dioecious — plant at least one male per five or six females to ensure pollination and fruit production; it grows naturally along Himalayan river valleys and tolerates poor, gravelly, or stony soils.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean soil — willow-leaved sea buckthorn tolerates poor, gravelly, or stony ground and is highly drought-tolerant once established, making it a superb windbreak tree for cold, exposed sites. Cold-stratify seeds for 30–60 days before sowing in early spring, or sow fresh seed in fall for natural stratification. Because the species is dioecious, include at least one male tree for every five to six female trees to ensure reliable pollination and fruit set; the dense orange berry clusters ripen in late summer to autumn and are extraordinarily rich in vitamin C, carotenoids, and omega-7 fatty acids. Larger and more tree-like than common sea buckthorn (H. rhamnoides), it can reach 30 feet, providing significant shade and windbreak value while producing a heavy fruit crop.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall after removing the waxy outer coating by rubbing in coarse sand or washing with a mild solvent, then allow natural cold stratification over winter; northern bayberry tolerates poor, sandy, acidic soil and salt spray, making it an outstanding native for coastal and disturbed sites.

directions: Sow seeds in fall after rubbing away the waxy seed coating, or provide a 60–90 day cold stratification period before spring sowing; northern bayberry grows naturally in coastal dunes, sandy thickets, and open woods from Newfoundland to North Carolina and thrives in lean, acidic, sandy or loamy soils with excellent drainage. This nitrogen-fixing native is highly tolerant of salt spray, wind, and drought, making it an exceptional choice for coastal landscapes and difficult sites where more refined shrubs fail. The aromatic, blue-gray waxy berries cluster tightly along the branches and persist into winter, historically rendered for bayberry candles; in the garden they provide excellent winter interest and food for birds such as yellow-rumped warblers. Northern bayberry spreads slowly by root suckers to form naturalistic colonies and requires both male and female plants for berry production.

planting.note: Take hardwood cuttings in late winter or softwood cuttings in early summer and root in well-drained propagation mix; plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun — multicaulis mulberry is vigorous and begins fruiting within two to three years of establishment.

directions: Propagate from hardwood cuttings taken in late winter and root in a well-drained propagation mix under plastic film, or take semi-softwood cuttings in early summer; plant rooted cuttings out in spring after the last frost in deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. Multicaulis mulberry is a vigorous, large-leaved variety of white mulberry selected in China for its enormous leaves — up to 12 inches long — that historically supported the silk industry; in the food garden it channels that same vigor into abundant, sweet white to pale-pink berries that ripen weeks ahead of most mulberries. The tree tolerates a wide range of soils including clay, heat, and urban conditions, and once established is drought-resilient while performing best with consistent moisture during fruiting. Harvest berries over several weeks as they ripen unevenly, spreading a sheet beneath the canopy and shaking branches gently to collect ripe fruit.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall immediately after collection for natural warm-then-cold stratification over winter; seeds require a period of warm followed by cold moist stratification to break dormancy, and germination may be slow and uneven over the first spring and into the second year — patience is essential.

directions: Sow fresh seed in fall as soon as it is collected, as broad-leaved linden requires a sequential warm then cold stratification period to break its compound dormancy; germination is slow and may extend into the second spring, so mark the planting site carefully and keep it weed-free. Plant in deep, fertile, moderately moist, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade, where the tree will develop a stately, broadly domed crown reaching 60 feet or more at maturity. In early summer the tree produces hanging clusters of fragrant creamy-yellow flowers along winged bracts that attract bees in enormous numbers — broad-leaved linden is considered one of the most important bee-forage trees in European temperate forestry and produces a prized light honey. Harvest flower clusters when fully open but before they begin to brown and dry them quickly in a warm, airy space; the dried flowers (tilleul) brew into a delicate, mildly sedative herbal tea with a long tradition in French and central European folk medicine.

planting.note: Sow directly in the garden in spring after the last frost, or start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; fig-leaved hollyhock is biennial, forming a leaf rosette the first year and sending up tall flowering spikes in the second — sow in successive years to ensure bloom every season.

directions: Sow seed directly in the garden after the last frost in spring, pressing seed shallowly into the surface of fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; fig-leaved hollyhock is a biennial that spends its first season as a ground-level rosette of deeply lobed, fig-shaped leaves before sending up tall flowering spikes the following summer. To maintain a continuous display, sow seed each year so that first-year rosettes and second-year blooming plants are always present side by side in the border. The single flowers in warm shades of yellow, apricot, orange, and pink open in succession from the base of the spike upward through midsummer, and the plant's naturally tough constitution makes it notably more resistant to hollyhock rust (Phragmidium malvacearum) than the common A. rosea. After the main spike sets seed in late summer, cut it back to encourage side shoots that may reflower before frost, and allow some seed to fall and self-sow for the following year's plants.
